_meta.note: BuzzFeed quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: Give and Take — Dopamine Master Quiz
html_007: “A successful Giver isn’t just a nice person.
html_008: They’re someone who knows ‘how to give.’”
html_010: Lock in the secrets of Givers, Takers, and Matchers — and what makes a ‘successful Giver’ — in
html_012:
.
        They start easy and ramp up,
html_013: and every one you miss comes back until you nail it.
html_030: That’s the end of your free preview
html_042: Give and Take at a glance
html_043: Thread the whole book onto one
html_045: and you’ll never forget it. Every path begins with
html_050: The habit of asking ‘what can I do for you?’ first
html_053: A window into other worlds → new chances & trust
html_056: Sharing knowledge · everyone’s a diamond in the rough
html_059: Others + self-interest / go Matcher mode so you’re never a pushover
html_062: “Compassion and ambition, at the same time”
html_064: Table of contents at a glance
html_066: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_068: Focused review of what you missed
html_070: — where you can watch Adam Grant give the talk himself.
html_075: A dopamine-fueled master quiz that drills the heart of the book

js_009: Adam Grant is an organizational psychology professor at Wharton. He was the school’s top-rated professor seven years running, and he studies motivation, generosity, and creativity at work through science.
js_011: What are the three types this book sorts people into?
js_016: The split comes down to how you handle reciprocity: Givers (who give more), Takers (who take more), and Matchers (who keep it even). These three interaction styles are the pillars of the whole book.
js_017: What’s the most basic trait of a Giver?
js_018: They like to give more than they get
js_019: They want back exactly what they gave
js_020: They work to get more than they give
js_021: They try to neither give nor take
js_022: A Giver meets you asking ‘how can I help you?’ first. They share time, knowledge, and information freely, without keeping tabs on what comes back.
js_023: What does a Taker ask first in any interaction?
js_024: What can you do for me?
js_025: How can the two of us help each other?
js_026: What can I do for you?
js_027: Is this relationship fair to everyone?
js_028: A Taker thinks ‘what can this person do for me?’ first. They see the world as a competition and put their own interests ahead of everyone else’s.
js_029: What core principle does a Matcher follow?
js_030: Fairness — give as you get, get as you give
js_031: Giving freely and expecting nothing back
js_032: Always grabbing the biggest share possible
js_034: A Matcher values fairness and reciprocity — ‘you help me, I help you.’ They keep a mental ledger of favors and debts to stay even. Most people at work are Matchers.
js_036: What’s the most surprising finding in this book?
js_037: Givers crowd the top of the ladder
js_040: Success has nothing to do with type
js_041: Against common sense, Givers are heavily represented at the very top of the success ladder. But they also crowd the very bottom — and that ‘paradox’ is the book’s starting point.
js_043: Why do so many Givers end up at the ‘bottom’ of the ladder?
js_044: They help others and never finish their own work
js_045: They simply lack the talent and ability
js_046: They were unusually unlucky and unfortunate
js_047: They had no ambition or goals to begin with
js_048: Givers who just pour it all out burn through their own time and energy and never deliver on their own goals. That’s why you see so many Givers at the bottom of the performance chart.
js_050: Among victims of scams and identity theft, which type showed up more?
js_051: Givers, at roughly twice the rate of Takers
js_054: All three types, in equal numbers
js_055: Givers tend to see the best in people first, so they get fooled more easily. In one study, Givers were twice as likely as Takers to be fraud victims. Being kind and being a pushover are two different things.
js_057: What negative emotion does Adam Grant say he keeps finding at work?
js_062: All over the workplace, Grant sees ‘paranoia’ (distrust) — and Takers are the source. Where Takers pile up, cooperation gets harder and the whole vibe turns toxic.
js_064: What’s the easiest self-check for whether you’re a Giver or a Taker?
js_065: Do I ask ‘what can I do for you?’ first?
js_066: How much smarter am I than everyone else?
js_067: How much money am I making each month?
js_068: How many close friends do I have right now?
js_069: If your first thought about a coworker is ‘what can I do for you?’ you lean Giver; if it’s ‘what can you do for me?’ you lean Taker.
js_074: As a psychologist, Grant explains success with ‘data’ — tracking thousands of people — rather than with ‘gut feeling.’
js_075: Careful — the three types aren’t fixed personalities but your ‘interaction default.’ They can shift with the situation.
js_076: Giver = ‘what can I do for you?’ Taker = ‘what can you do for me?’ That one question splits the two.
js_077: Takers see the world as eat-or-be-eaten and cling to proving how capable they are.
js_078: It matters that Matchers are the ‘majority’ — which is why Takers eventually get taken down by them.
js_079: Givers cluster at ‘both extremes’ — the top and the bottom of the ladder. What separates the two is the heart of the book.
js_080: The failure pattern = losing control of time and energy. Without ‘boundaries’ on their giving, Givers fall apart.
js_081: Agreeableness and being a Giver are separate things. There are gruff Givers and charming Takers.
js_082: Grant’s warning: a single Taker can turn a whole team’s vibe ‘toxic.’
js_083: The self-assessment is only accurate if you answer ‘honestly.’ You can try it yourself at giveandtake.com.
js_097: Someone who asks ‘what can I do for you?’ first. They share time, knowledge, and information with no strings attached.
js_099: Someone who asks ‘what can you do for me?’ first. They put their own interests ahead of others.
js_101: The majority type — they value fairness and reciprocity, giving as they get and getting as they give.
js_103: A Giver who helps others while looking after their own interests — the secret to reaching the top of the ladder.
js_104: Not-so-close ties that link you to other worlds — your channel to fresh chances and information.
js_106: The habit of giving small, often, and low-cost — protecting yourself while adding big value (Rifkin).
js_108: When Givers meet the people their work helps, their motivation explodes and burnout drops.
js_109: When giving becomes the norm, even Takers and Matchers turn generous. That’s the power of system design.
js_111: Why nice people finish last — Givers, Takers, and Matchers
js_112: There are three types in the world: ‘Givers’ who give more than they get, ‘Takers’ who angle for more, and ‘Matchers’ who keep the balance.
js_113: Who climbs to the top of the success ladder? Common sense says ‘Takers’ — the data says otherwise.
js_114: First, a shocker — the ‘bottom’ of the ladder is full of Givers too. They gave so much they never finished their own work.
js_115: And yet the ‘top’ is full of Givers as well. Same type — so why the opposite results?
js_116: The whole book is a journey to crack that difference. The key is ‘how you give.’
js_118: The Giver’s network and collaboration — weak ties, and growing the pie
js_119: Givers thrive in ‘weak-tie’ networks. New chances come from loose connections, not close friends.
js_120: When they revive ‘dormant ties’ they lost touch with long ago, Givers get help freely — thanks to the trust they’ve banked over the years.
js_121: Givers shine in collaboration too. They take on the thankless jobs and share knowledge, growing the pie for the whole team.
js_122: Takers try to grab a bigger slice of a fixed pie; Givers grow the pie itself so everyone benefits.
js_123: One Google engineer earned eight peer bonuses in a single year — in a Giver culture, giving comes back fast.
js_125: How Giver leaders see — spotting potential and the motivation of impact
js_126: Giver leaders see ‘everyone as a diamond, it’s all in the cutting.’ They believe in potential and invest in it.
js_127: Taker leaders, by contrast, ‘back only the sure things’ and give up early on anyone who doesn’t look promising.
js_128: Givers work harder and longer — but only when they can see the ‘meaning’ in their work.
js_129: When fundraising call-center staff met a student their scholarships supported, their call time and donations jumped.
js_130: The moment they see that ‘my work helps someone,’ a Giver’s energy stops running dry.
js_132: How not to be a pushover — the Giver’s self-defense
js_133: A Giver’s biggest risk is becoming a Taker’s ‘pushover.’
js_134: Givers have a positive bias and trust people easily — which is why they were twice as common among fraud victims.
js_135: The fix is to act ‘like a Matcher.’ With a Taker, use generous tit-for-tat — be generous only once in every three moves.
js_136: One more thing — Givers struggle to argue for themselves. Reframe it as ‘negotiating on someone else’s behalf’ and they get tougher.
js_137: The key isn’t to be a self-sacrificing ‘selfless Giver,’ but a ‘pro-social Giver’ who looks after themselves too.
js_139: From self-sacrificing Giver to successful Giver — the art of self-care
js_140: The all-out ‘selfless Giver’ runs out of energy and eventually collapses (burnout).
js_141: Successful Givers are ‘pro-social’ — they help others while protecting their own goals and health.
js_142: ‘Chunking’ your giving — bundling help into one block instead of scattering it raises both efficiency and satisfaction.
js_143: ‘Asking’ for help is part of a Giver culture too — giving only circulates when people can safely ask for it.
js_144: Protect the Giver, and they can give more, for longer. Self-care isn’t selfishness.
js_146: Toward a world where Givers win
js_147: In organizations where giving is the ‘norm,’ even Takers and Matchers turn generous. Culture changes people.
js_148: The reciprocity ring — lower the risk of giving, and design systems where giving is fun and rewarding.
js_149: The one-line conclusion: successful Givers hold ‘compassion’ and ‘ambition’ at the same time.
js_150: As Grant puts it — ‘I want to live in a world where Givers succeed.’
js_151: A sharp reader holds onto this powerful argument while remembering its limit — the neatness of a three-way split.
js_153: Rifkin, a successful Silicon Valley founder, helped countless people with his ‘five-minute favor’ philosophy. His secret: ‘you don’t have to be Mother Teresa — small acts of value can be a big help.’
js_154: It shows that giving doesn’t have to be grand — and that small favors build into a huge network of trust.
js_155: The call center and the scholarship student
js_156: When fundraising call-center staff met a ‘scholarship student’ funded by the money they raised, their call time and donations shot up dramatically.
js_157: It’s proof that seeing your impact — ‘my work helps someone’ — sets off an explosion in motivation and performance.
js_159: The worst-performing salespeople and the best-performing ones were all ‘Givers.’ The difference was whether they looked after their own goals too. The top Givers pulled in 50% more revenue than average.
js_160: Key evidence that even among Givers, being ‘pro-social’ versus ‘selfless’ decides success or failure.
js_161: By default, which do I ask first — ‘what can I do for you?’ or ‘what can you do for me?’
js_162: Look at the ‘default’ of your interactions and you’ll see whether you lean Giver, Taker, or Matcher.
js_163: Am I giving while still taking good enough care of ‘myself’?
js_164: Selfless Givers burn out; pro-social Givers last. Self-care isn’t selfishness — it’s strategy.
js_165: Is my team or organization a culture where giving is ‘safe and rewarding,’ or one of competition and distrust?
js_166: Culture changes the individual. A world where Givers succeed starts with system design.
js_167: You’ve completely nailed Give and Take
js_168: You’ve got almost all of the core logic
js_170: You’ve got the framework — one more round!
js_172: The more you repeat, the higher you score

_app.desc: Master Adam Grant’s Givers, Takers, and Matchers — 30 quizzes and one Reading Report.
